skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X actions through the inference.sh belt CLI, including posting, media posts, likes, retweets, DMs, follows, profile lookup, and post lookup.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X publishing and engagement workflows with belt CLI commands.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: The weak cryptography detections at SKILL.md lines 3 and 27 are false positives; no cryptographic code is present. The external command findings are Bash examples and belt CLI usage, not Ruby backtick execution, but the skill can publish, delete, message, follow, like, and retweet through a network service. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it creates meaningful account-action risk and should be published with clear warnings.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Social Account Actions Through CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill permits belt CLI commands that can post tweets, create media posts, like posts, retweet, delete posts, send direct messages, follow users, and query profiles. These actions are expected for the skill, but mistakes or prompt misuse could affect a connected Twitter/X account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed belt commands are explicit and match the skill purpose. The risk is account-impacting automation, not command injection or hidden malware.

medium_findings.title: External Network Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill depends on inference.sh documentation, hosted media, and belt app calls to reach external services. This is expected for Twitter/X automation, but users should understand that requests leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs and CLI integration clearly require external network access. No evidence found of covert exfiltration beyond the advertised service integration.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at SKILL.md lines 3 and 27, but those lines contain metadata text and an application table header. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not implement or invoke cryptography. The match appears to come from ordinary text around Twitter/X metadata and table labels.

low_findings.title: External Command Scanner Label Is Overbroad
low_findings.description: The scanner labeled many fenced bash examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file contains documentation examples for belt commands, not Ruby backtick syntax or hidden shell wrappers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are plain bash snippets and documented belt invocations. The real concern is authorized account automation, which is covered separately.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Bash Allowance For belt
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed tool declaration permits Bash execution of belt commands. This is necessary for the skill, but it allows account-changing operations when the user is authenticated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit and directly enables the documented CLI workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Social Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents external service calls for posting, liking, retweeting, messaging, following, and deleting content on Twitter/X.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct examples of account-affecting network automation. No evidence found of hidden or unrelated command execution.

content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X actions by hand can slow content teams and solo creators. This skill provides repeatable belt CLI workflows for publishing, engagement, messaging, and profile lookup.
actual_capabilities: Creates media posts through x/post-create using a media URL.
Likes, retweets, and deletes posts when given post identifiers.
Sends direct messages and follows users through dedicated X apps.
Retrieves user profiles and post details for review workflows.
Shows workflows for posting AI-generated images or videos.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Actions depend on connected Twitter/X permissions and platform limits.
The skill documents commands; it does not provide approval workflows.
Media posting requires a reachable media URL.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Prepare and post short campaign updates from a repeatable command workflow.

use_cases.title: Manage Creator Engagement
use_cases.description: Like, retweet, follow, and message users from a consistent CLI process.

use_cases.title: Test X Integration Flows
use_cases.description: Run profile, post, media, and deletion commands while validating automation behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Twitter automation skill to help me post this text: [tweet text]. Show the exact belt command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.scenario: Post text with an image or video URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Twitter/X media post with this message: [message]. Use this media URL: [URL]. Ask for confirmation before posting.

prompt_templates.title: Review Engagement Actions
prompt_templates.scenario: Like, retweet, or follow with approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare engagement commands for these targets: [post IDs or usernames]. Separate likes, retweets, and follows, then wait for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Publishing Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Twitter/X workflow for [campaign]. Include post creation, optional media generation, profile checks, and deletion rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Post a launch announcement with a product image URL.
output_examples.output: A concise tweet draft, a media post command plan, and a confirmation checkpoint before publishing.

output_examples.input: Prepare engagement for three post IDs and two usernames.
output: A grouped action list for likes, retweets, and follows.
The required identifiers for each action.
A reminder to confirm before account-changing commands run.

output_examples.input: Get details for a user and a post before replying.
output_examples.output: A lookup plan using user and post commands, followed by a short summary of what to review before responding.

best_practices: Confirm every posting, deletion, DM, follow, like, and retweet before execution.
Use test accounts or low-risk posts when validating new workflows.
Keep tweet text, media URLs, and target IDs in reviewed input files.
anti_patterns: Do not run bulk engagement commands without rate-limit and policy review.
Do not send DMs or follow users from unverified generated lists.
Do not paste secrets or access tokens into tweet text or command input.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the inference.sh belt CLI and connected X apps to run account actions.

faq.question: Does it require login?
faq.answer: Yes. You need the belt CLI and an authenticated account with the right X integration permissions.

faq.question: Can it post images or videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The media workflow uses x/post-create with a reachable media URL.

faq.question: Can it delete tweets?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented x/post-delete command can delete a post when given a valid post ID.

faq.question: Is it safe for automatic engagement bots?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review platform rules, rate limits, and target lists before running engagement actions.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
